narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.60 and an LSAT score of 39, you've clearly put in strong effort to build a competitive profile for law school. Your family comes from a middle-income background, you're planning to attend full-time, and your school deciles show you ranked in the 5th percentile for Year 1 grades but improved to the 6th in Year 3. The admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on profiles like yours, estimated a 63.8% chance that you would fail the bar—above the threshold that led to your application's rejection. This decision hinged most heavily on your family income bracket, which nudged the prediction toward failure more than any other factor in your case. Your Year 1 decile ranking was the next biggest influence pushing in the same concerning direction, suggesting early academic struggles carried significant weight. Interestingly, your LSAT score and undergraduate GPA each played smaller roles in tipping toward a failure prediction, while your Year 3 decile improvement was one of the few elements that pulled things back toward passing. Being full-time, like most applicants, had virtually no sway. Compared to typical students, your LSAT and GPA stand out as stronger than average, and your decile progress is encouraging, but the model weighed your middle-income status and initial Year 1 performance as bigger risks relative to peers who passed the bar. While this outcome is disappointing, it doesn't define your potential—many overcome similar hurdles. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT for an even higher score, bolstering early-grade explanations in a reapplications essay, or gaining relevant experience to strengthen your narrative. We encourage you to reapply after addressing these areas, as targeted improvements could shift the model's view dramatically. You're capable of success, and this is just one data point on your journey. Keep pushing forward.
